category: Integration Patterns for Mid-Market IT Landscape
description: Common integration patterns and architectural approaches for connecting ERP, DMS, CRM, and third-party systems in mid-market companies. Includes pattern descriptions, use cases, technology options, complexity ratings, and trade-offs.

patterns.name: Point-to-Point Integration
patterns.description: Direct connection between two systems using API, file transfer, or database link. Each integration is custom-built for the specific pair of systems.
use_cases: Simple, stable integrations between two systems unlikely to change (e.g., ERP to bank)
Low-volume data exchange where middleware overhead is not justified
One-time data migration between systems
patterns.complexity: Low per integration; High at scale (n*(n-1)/2 connections for n systems)
pros: Simple to implement for single integration
No middleware cost
Fast initial delivery
cons: Creates 'spaghetti architecture' as system count grows
Any system change can break multiple integrations
No centralized monitoring or error handling
Difficult to maintain as team knowledge becomes siloed
patterns.recommended_for: 1-3 system integrations only; avoid as primary strategy for complex landscapes

patterns.name: Hub-and-Spoke (Enterprise Service Bus / iPaaS)
patterns.description: Centralized integration platform (ESB or iPaaS) acts as hub. All systems connect to the hub using standardized connectors. Message transformation, routing, and orchestration handled centrally.
use_cases: ERP as master data hub with multiple satellite systems (CRM, WMS, e-commerce)
B2B EDI/API integration with suppliers and customers
Multi-system workflows spanning ERP, DMS, and approval systems
technology_options: Microsoft Azure Integration Services (Azure Logic Apps + Service Bus + API Management)
Zapier / Make (Integromat) for simple mid-market scenarios
patterns.complexity: Medium-High to implement; Low to maintain at scale
pros: Centralized monitoring and error alerting
Reusable connectors reduce future integration effort by 60-70%
Single place to change when a system API changes
Supports complex routing and transformation logic
Enables event-driven architecture
cons: Middleware cost: €1,500-€5,000/month for mid-market iPaaS
Requires dedicated integration developer/administrator
Initial setup takes 4-12 weeks before first integration goes live
Can become a bottleneck if not properly sized
patterns.recommended_for: Companies with 4+ systems requiring integration; standard choice for mid-market digital transformation

patterns.name: ERP as System of Record (Master Data Management)
patterns.description: ERP is designated as the master source of truth for key business data (customers, suppliers, products, chart of accounts). All other systems receive data from ERP; changes originate in ERP only.
use_cases: Customer master data managed in ERP, synchronized to CRM and DMS
Supplier master data managed in ERP, synchronized to procurement portal and DMS
Chart of accounts in ERP used as reference in BI and reporting tools
technology_options: ERP native export/API (SAP Business One Service Layer, BC REST API)
Master Data Management (MDM) platform for complex scenarios
patterns.complexity: Medium; increases with data quality issues in legacy systems
pros: Single source of truth eliminates data inconsistencies
Simplifies compliance (GDPR data subject requests easier when data is centralized)
Reduces reconciliation effort at month-end
cons: Data quality in ERP must be excellent before propagating to other systems
Requires change management: staff must update ERP first, not CRM or spreadsheets
Conflict resolution needed when multiple systems need write access to same data
patterns.recommended_for: All mid-market companies; should be established as governance policy at project start

patterns.name: Event-Driven Integration (Webhooks and Message Queues)
patterns.description: Systems publish events when business objects change. Subscribing systems react to events in near real-time. Uses message broker or webhook pattern for asynchronous, loosely-coupled integration.
use_cases: Invoice posted in ERP → trigger DMS archiving workflow automatically
Order received in e-commerce → trigger ERP order creation
Document approved in DMS → trigger payment release in ERP
Shipment confirmed in WMS → trigger customer invoice creation in ERP
patterns.complexity: Medium-High; requires understanding of event sourcing and eventual consistency
pros: Near real-time data synchronization (seconds vs. hourly batch jobs)
Resilient: message queues buffer events during system downtime
Decoupled: publishing system doesn't need to know about subscribers
Scales well for high event volumes
cons: Eventual consistency: systems may briefly show different data states
Debugging event-driven flows is harder than synchronous calls
Requires message schema versioning strategy
Not all mid-market ERP systems support outbound webhooks natively
patterns.recommended_for: Automated document processing pipelines; time-sensitive workflows (approvals, notifications)

patterns.name: File-Based Batch Integration (SFTP / AS2 / EDI)
patterns.description: Systems exchange structured data files (CSV, XML, EDIFACT, X12) at scheduled intervals. Traditional approach, still dominant in B2B and legacy system integration.
use_cases: Supplier EDI (EDIFACT ORDERS, DESADV, INVOIC) for high-volume procurement
Bank statement imports (MT940, CAMT.053) into ERP for bank reconciliation
Legacy ERP exports to modern cloud analytics platform
E-invoicing file submission (KSeF FA(2) XML, XRechnung UBL)
technology_options: EDI gateway: SPS Commerce, TrueCommerce, Cleo Integration Cloud
AS2 protocol for secure B2B file exchange (EDI standard)
Azure Blob Storage / AWS S3 triggers for cloud-native file processing
patterns.complexity: Low-Medium; EDI mapping can be complex for EDIFACT
pros: Battle-tested for B2B integration; all major suppliers support EDI
Can handle large data volumes efficiently
Simple error recovery: re-process failed file
Low cost for simple CSV/XML file exchange
cons: Batch latency: data is hours old at best
File format mapping requires specialized EDI expertise
SFTP security management (key rotation, access control) adds operational overhead
Not suitable for real-time workflows
patterns.recommended_for: B2B EDI with suppliers/customers; bank reconciliation; legacy system bridges

patterns.name: API Gateway Pattern
patterns.description: Centralized API gateway manages, secures, and monitors all API traffic between internal systems and external parties. Provides authentication, rate limiting, request transformation, and API lifecycle management.
use_cases: Exposing ERP data to customer portal via secured REST API
Managing KSeF / XRechnung API calls through a resilient gateway with retry logic
Supplier portal accessing inventory availability via rate-limited API
Mobile app accessing business data with OAuth 2.0 security
patterns.complexity: Medium; significantly reduces complexity for external-facing APIs
pros: Centralized security enforcement (OAuth 2.0, API keys, mutual TLS)
Rate limiting protects backend systems from overload
API versioning without breaking existing consumers
Centralized monitoring, logging, and analytics for all API calls
Enables API monetization if needed
cons: Additional infrastructure component to maintain
Adds latency (typically 5-15ms)
Requires API design expertise
Overkill for purely internal integrations
patterns.recommended_for: Any organization exposing APIs to external parties (customers, suppliers, government systems)
